samples.xml_description:
<module id="serial2parallel">
    <description>Implement a series-parallel conversion circuit.</description>
    <ports>
        <input id="clk">Clock signal.</input>
        <input id="rst_n">Reset signal (active low).</input>
        <input id="din_serial">Serial input data.</input>
        <input id="din_valid">Validity signal for input data.</input>
        <output id="dout_parallel" width_description="[7:0]">Parallel output data.</output>
        <output id="dout_valid">Validity signal for the output data.</output>
    </ports>
    <parameter_description>
        <parameter name="cnt">Counter to count the number of input data.</parameter>
    </parameter_description>
    <logic_description>
        <reg type="sequential_logic" id="cnt" width_description="[3:0]">Counter to count the number of input data.</reg>
    </logic_description>
    <implementation>
        <case>
            <item>
                <condition>
                    <and>
                        <item><signal>reset</signal></item>
                    </and>
                </condition>
                <action>
                    <sequential>
                        <item><signal>cnt</signal> = <value>0</value>;</item>
                        <item><signal>dout_valid</signal> = <value>0</value>;</item>
                    </sequential>
                </action>
            </item>
            <item>
                <condition>
                    <and>
                        <item><signal>reset</signal> is not active</item>
                        <item><signal>cnt</signal> == <value>8</value></item>
                    </and>
                </condition>
                <action>
                    <sequential>
                        <item><signal>cnt</signal> = <value>0</value>;</item>
                        <item><signal>dout_valid</signal> = <value>1</value>;</item>
                    </sequential>
                </action>
            </item>
            <item>
                <condition>
                    <and>
                        <item><signal>reset</signal> is not active</item>
                        <item><signal>cnt</signal> != <value>8</value></item>
                    </and>
                </condition>
                <action>
                    <sequential>
                        <item><signal>cnt</signal> = <signal>cnt</signal> + <value>1</value>;</item>
                        <item><signal>dout_valid</signal> = <value>0</value>;</item>
                    </sequential>
                </action>
            </item>
        </case>
    </implementation>
</module>

samples.xml_description:
Design a simplified radix-2 divider on 8-bit numbers. Clock signal. Reset signal. Signed or unsigned operation. Input operand DIVIDEND. Input operand DIVISOR. Valid operation request. The output has been acknowledged. The result is valid. Output result. 0 1 2 3 4 5 Counter for division process. Start flag for division process. Negated divisor. Shift register for division process.

When

opn_valid

is high and

res_valid

is low,

dividend

and

divisor

are saved.

The shift register

sr

is initialized to the absolute value of the dividend shifted left by one bit.

The

neg_divisor

is set to the negated absolute value of the divisor.

The counter

cnt

is set to 1 and

start_cnt

to 1 to begin the division process.

When

start_cnt

is high, the module performs the following steps:

  1. When

    cnt

    reaches 8 (most significant bit of

    cnt

    is set), the division is complete.

    • cnt

      and

      start_cnt

      are cleared.

    • The shift register

      sr

      is updated with the final remainder and quotient.

  2. Otherwise,

    cnt

    is incremented, and the shift register

    sr

    is updated based on the subtraction result:

    • Subtracts

      neg_divisor.

    • The multiplexer selects the appropriate result based on the carry-out.

    • The shift register

      sr

      is updated by shifting left and inserting the carry-out.

When

res_valid

is high and

res_ready

is low,

res_valid

is cleared.

samples.xml_description:
<module id="width_8to16">
    <description>Converts 8-bit data input to 16-bit data output.</description>
    <ports>
        <input id="clk">Clock signal.</input>
        <input id="rst_n">Active-low reset signal.</input>
        <input id="valid_in">Input signal indicating the validity of the input data.</input>
        <input id="data_in" width_description="[7:0]">8-bit input data.</input>
        <output id="valid_out">Output signal indicating the validity of the output data.</output>
        <output id="data_out" width_description="[15:0]">16-bit output data.</output>
    </ports>
    <parameter_description>
        <enum id="flag">Flag signal for controlling data output.</enum>
    </parameter_description>
    <logic_description>
        <reg type="sequential_logic" id="data_lock" width_description="[7:0]">Register for temporarily storing the first valid input data.</reg>
        <reg type="sequential_logic" id="flag">Flag signal for controlling data output.</reg>
    </logic_description>
    <implementation>
        <when test="&rst_n">
            <block>
                <case test="&valid_in">
                    <statement test="!flag">data_out <= {data_lock, data_in};</statement>
                    <statement test="flag">valid_out <= 1'b1;</statement>
                </case>
                <case test="&valid_in">
                    <statement test="!flag">flag <= 1'b1;</statement>
                    <statement test="flag">flag <= 1'b0;</statement>
                </case>
                <case test="!&valid_in">
                    <statement test="flag">valid_out <= 1'b1;</statement>
                </case>
            </block>
        </when>
        <else>
            <block>
                <case test="!flag">
                    <statement test="!valid_in">data_out <= 16'd0;</statement>
                </case>
                <case test="flag">
                    <statement test="!valid_in">valid_out <= 1'b0;</statement>
                </case>
            </block>
        </else>
    </implementation>
</module>

<module id="pe">
    <description>Implement a Multiplying Accumulator for 32bit integer.</description>
    <ports>
        <input id="clk">Clock.</input>
        <input id="rst">Reset signal. Defined as 1 for reset and 0 for reset signal inactive.</input>
        <input id="a" width_description="[31:0]">32-bit input operand A.</input>
        <input id="b" width_description="[31:0]">32-bit input operand B.</input>
        <output id="c" width_description="[31:0]">32-bit output representing the accumulated result.</output>
    </ports>
    <parameter_description>
        <param id="MAC_PE">This is a multiplier-accumulator.</param>
    </parameter_description>
    <implementation>Implement a multiplier-accumulator using a parallel architecture. Use a register to store the partial sum (intermediate accumulation result) and add the product of the input operands to it in each clock cycle.</implementation>
</module>

samples.xml_description:
<module id="traffic_light">
    <description>Implement a traffic light with red, yellow, green indicators and a pedestrian button. In normal traffic light operation, the east-west (EW) cycle is 60 clock cycles of green, 5 clock cycles of yellow, 10 clock cycles of red, and the north-south (NS) cycle is 10 clock cycles of green, 5 clock cycles of yellow, 60 clock cycles of red. The pedestrian button, when pressed, reduces the remaining green time to 10 clocks if the remaining time is greater than 10 clocks. If the remaining time is less than 10 clocks, the green time is not extended and the pedestrian red light remains on. The lane light and the sidewalk light are paired: when the lane light is green or yellow, the sidewalk light is red; when the lane light is red, the sidewalk light is green.</description>
    <ports>
        <input id="rst_n">Reset signal (active low).</input>
        <input id="clk">Clock signal.</input>
        <input id="pass_request">Pedestrian button input.</input>
        <output id="red">Red output signal.</output>
        <output id="yellow">Yellow output signal.</output>
        <output id="green">Green output signal.</output>
    </ports>
    <parameter_description>
        <enum name="idle">Idle state.</enum>
        <enum name="s1_red">State 1 red.</enum>
        <enum name="s2_yellow">State 2 yellow.</enum>
        <enum name="s3_green">State 3 green.</enum>
    </parameter_description>
    <logic_description>
        <sequential_logic>
            <reg id="cnt" type="8">Internal counter.</reg>
            <reg id="state" type="2">Current state.</reg>
            <reg id="p_red">Previous red signal.</reg>
            <reg id="p_yellow">Previous yellow signal.</reg>
            <reg id="p_green">Previous green signal.</reg>
            <always @(*)>
                <case expr="state">
                    <item value="idle">
                        <assign id="state" value="s1_red" />
                        <assign id="p_red" value="0" />
                        <assign id="p_yellow" value="0" />
                        <assign id="p_green" value="0" />
                    </item>
                    <item value="s1_red">
                        <assign id="p_red" value="1" />
                        <assign id="p_yellow" value="0" />
                        <assign id="p_green" value="0" />
                        <if expr="cnt == 3">
                            <assign id="state" value="s3_green" />
                        </if>
                        <else>
                            <assign id="state" value="s1_red" />
                        </else>
                    </item>
                    <item value="s2_yellow">
                        <assign id="p_red" value="0" />
                        <assign id="p_yellow" value="1" />
                        <assign id="p_green" value="0" />
                        <if expr="cnt == 3">
                            <assign id="state" value="s1_red" />
                        </if>
                        <else>
                            <assign id="state" value="s2_yellow" />
                        </else>
                    </item>
                    <item value="s3_green">
                        <assign id="p_red" value="0" />
                        <assign id="p_yellow" value="0" />
                        <assign id="p_green" value="1" />
                        <if expr="cnt == 3">
                            <assign id="state" value="s2_yellow" />
                        </if>
                        <else>
                            <assign id="state" value="s3_green" />
                        </else>
                    </item>
                </case>
            </always>
            <always @(posedge clk or negedge rst_n)>
                <if expr="!rst_n">
                    <assign id="cnt" value="10" />
                </if>
                <else>
                    <if expr="pass_request && green">
                        <assign id="cnt" value="10" />
                    </if>
                    <else>
                        <if expr="!green && p_green">
                            <assign id="cnt" value="60" />
                        </if>
                        <else>
                            <if expr="!yellow && p_yellow">
                                <assign id="cnt" value="5" />
                            </if>
                            <else>
                                <if expr="!red && p_red">
                                    <assign id="cnt" value="10" />
                                </if>
                                <else>
                                    <assign id="cnt" value="cnt - 1" />
                                </else>
                            </else>
                        </end>
                    </else>
                </end>
            </always>
            <assign id="clock" value="cnt" />
        </sequential_logic>
        <sequential_logic>
            <always @(posedge clk or negedge rst_n)>
                <assign id="red" value="p_red" />
                <assign id="yellow" value="p_yellow" />
                <assign id="green" value="p_green" />
            </always>
        </sequential_logic>
    </logic_description>
</module>

It maintains a shift count (i) to control the multiplication process and a completion flag (done_r) to indicate when the multiplication is done. On each clock cycle, if the reset signal is active, the shift count and completion flag are reset to zero. If the shift count is less than 17 and the start signal is active, the shift count increments by one. samples.xml_description:
Implement an 8-bit unsigned multiplier using a pipelined approach. Clock signal for synchronization. Active-low reset signal. Input enable signal for multiplication. Multiplicand input. Multiplier input. Output enable signal for multiplication. Product output. 8 8 The module multi_pipe_8bit implements an 8-bit unsigned multiplier using a pipelined architecture. It takes two 8-bit inputs, mul_a and mul_b, and produces a 16-bit output mul_out.

The module has an input enable signal mul_en_in that controls the multiplication operation. The output enable signal mul_en_out indicates whether the multiplication operation is enabled.

To achieve pipelining, the module uses multiple clock cycles to perform the multiplication operation. It consists of four stages:
  1. Input control: The module samples the input enable signal and the input data on the positive edge of the clock.
  2. Partial product generation: The module generates partial products by performing bitwise operations between the multiplicand and the individual bits of the multiplier.
  3. Partial sum calculation: The module performs addition operations on the partial products to calculate partial sums.
  4. Final product calculation: The module sums up all the partial sums to obtain the final product.
The final product is available on the output mul_out. The module is reset to zero when the reset signal rst_n is active.

<module id="parallel2serial">
    <description>Convert 4 parallel input bits into a serial output.</description>
    <ports>
        <input id="clk">Clock.</input>
        <input id="rst_n">Active-low reset.</input>
        <input id="d" width_description="[3:0]">4-bit parallel input.</input>
        <output id="valid_out">Valid signal for serial output.</output>
        <output id="dout">Serial output bit.</output>
    </ports>
    <parameter_description>
        <parameter name="N" value="4">Width of data input.</parameter>
        <parameter name="WIDTH" value="1">Width of serial output.</parameter>
    </parameter_description>
    <logic_description>
        <reg id="cnt" type="sequential_logic" width_description="[2:0]">Counter register for serial output.</reg>
        <reg id="data" type="sequential_logic" width_description="[3:0]">Shift register for parallel input.</reg>
        <reg id="valid" type="sequential_logic">Valid signal for serial output.</reg>
    </logic_description>
    <implementation>
        In serial output, the most significant bit of the parallel input is output first, followed by the remaining bits.
        If the counter is 3 (indicating the last bit), the parallel input is loaded into the shift register, and the counter is reset to 0.
        Otherwise, the counter is incremented, and the shift register is shifted left by 1 bit.
    </implementation>
</module>

Diversify FSMs. Distinguish FSMs by their high-level properties. Avoid structural descriptions. Input. Clock. Reset. Output. IDLE S1 S10 S101 S1011 State register. next_state Output register. next_match

Describe the algorithm inline, in prose.

For example, the FSM for

is described as

  • Four states: IDLE, A, B, C.
  • State transition in always @(*).
  • Output in always @(posedge CLK).

Reset state to IDLE.

Transition from IDLE to A on in=1'b1.

Transition from A to B on in=1'b0.

Transition from B to C on in=1'b1.

Transition from C to IDLE on in=1'b0.

Output 1 in state C.

Output 0 in other states.

samples.xml_description: Design a frequency divider that divides the input clock frequency of 100MHz into 50MHz, 10MHz, and 1MHz. Input clock signal Reset signal Output clock signal with a frequency of CLK_in divided by 2. Output clock signal with a frequency of CLK_in divided by 10. Output clock signal with a frequency of CLK_in divided by 100. Input clock signal Reset signal Output clock signal with a frequency of CLK_in divided by 2. Output clock signal with a frequency of CLK_in divided by 10. Output clock signal with a frequency of CLK_in divided by 100. Reset the counter on the positive edge of RST or the negative edge of CLK_in. If RST is active, set CLK_50 to 0. Otherwise, toggle CLK_50. Set CLK_50 to 1 if RST is active. Set CLK_50 to the negation of CLK_50 if RST is not active. Reset the counter on the positive edge of RST or the negative edge of CLK_in. If RST is active, set CLK_10 to 0 and the counter cnt_10 to 0. Otherwise, increment the counter cnt_10. Set CLK_10 to 1 if RST is active. Set the counter cnt_10 to 0 if RST is active. Set the counter cnt_10 to the negation of cnt_10 if RST is not active and the counter cnt_10 reaches a value of 4, set CLK_10 to the negation of CLK_10. Reset the counter on the positive edge of RST or the negative edge of CLK_in. If RST is active, set CLK_1 to 0 and the counter cnt_100 to 0. Otherwise, increment the counter cnt_100. Set CLK_1 to 1 if RST is active. Set the counter cnt_100 to 0 if RST is active. Set the counter cnt_100 to the negation of cnt_100 if RST is not active and the counter cnt_100 reaches a value of 49, set CLK_1 to the negation of CLK_1. Implement the frequency divider using counters. Follow the implementation of the counter_50, counter_10, and counter_1. Reset the counter on the positive edge of RST or the negative edge of CLK_in. If RST is active, set CLK_50 to 0. Otherwise, toggle CLK_50. always @(posedge CLK_in or posedge RST) begin if (RST) begin CLK_50 <= 1'b0; end else begin CLK_50 <= ~CLK_50; end end Reset the counter on the positive edge of RST or the negative edge of CLK_in. If RST is active, set CLK_10 to 0 and the counter cnt_10 to 0. Otherwise, increment the counter cnt_10. always @(posedge CLK_in or posedge RST) begin if (RST) begin cnt_10 <= 4'b0000; CLK_10 <= 1'b0; end else if (cnt_10 == 4'b0100) begin CLK_10 <= ~CLK_10; cnt_10 <= 4'b0000; end else begin cnt_10 <= cnt_10 + 1; end end Reset the counter on the positive edge of RST or the negative edge of CLK_in. If RST is active, set CLK_1 to 0 and the counter cnt_100 to 0. Otherwise, increment the counter cnt_100. always @(posedge CLK_in or posedge RST) begin if (RST) begin cnt_100 <= 6'b000000; CLK_1 <= 1'b0; end else if (cnt_100 == 6'b001100) begin CLK_1 <= ~CLK_1; cnt_100 <= 6'b000000; end else begin cnt_100 <= cnt_100 + 1; end end

samples.xml_description: Implement an ALU for a 32-bit MIPS-ISA CPU. a 32-bit input operand. a 32-bit input operand. a 6-bit control signal for selecting the operation to be performed. a 32-bit output representing the result of the operation. a 1-bit output indicating whether the result is zero. a 1-bit output indicating whether a carry occurred during the operation. a 1-bit output indicating whether the result is negative. a 1-bit output indicating whether an overflow occurred during the operation. a 1-bit output representing a general flag, which is set based on specific operations (SLT and SLTU). ADD ADDU SUB SUBU AND OR XOR NOR SLT SLTU SLL SRL SRA SLLV SRLV SRAV LUI Uses parameters to define the control signals for various operations, such as ADD, SUB, AND, OR, etc. Uses case to select the operation to be performed based on the control signal aluc. The module assigns the input operands to the signed wires and the output result r to the lower 32 bits of the register res[31:0]. The flag output is determined based on the control signal aluc and is set to '1' when the operation is SLT or SLTU, and 'z' (high-impedance) otherwise. The zero output is set to '1' when the result is all zeros, and '0' otherwise. Inside the always block, a case statement is used to perform the appropriate operation based on the control signal aluc. For shift operations (SLL, SRL, SRA, SLLV, SRLV, SRAV), the shift amount is determined by the value of a or a[4:0]. For the LUI operation, the upper 16 bits of a are concatenated with 16 zeros to form the result. If the control signal aluc does not match any defined operation, the result is assigned as 'z' (high-impedance).

samples.xml_description:
Implement a 4-bit unsigned number pipeline multiplier. Clock. Active-low reset. Multiplicand. Multiplier. Product. 4 The input signals (mul_a and mul_b) are extended by adding "size" number of zero bits at the most significant bit positions.
For example, if the input signal mul_a is 4'b1010, the extended signal mul_a_extend is 8'b00001010.
The multiplication operation is performed using a generate block, which generates the partial products based on the multiplier bits.
For each bit position i from 0 to size-1, the partial product is calculated as follows:
If the corresponding bit in the multiplier is 1, the multiplicand is left-shifted by i positions.
If the corresponding bit in the multiplier is 0, the partial product is set to 0 ('d0).
The partial products are then added to obtain the final product.
The module uses registers to store the intermediate sum values.
On the positive edge of the clock signal (clk) or the falling edge of the reset signal (rst_n), the module performs add operations.
If the reset signal (rst_n) is low, indicating a reset condition, the registers are set to 0.
If the reset signal (rst_n) is high, the registers are updated with the sum of the corresponding partial products.
On the positive edge of the clock signal (clk) or the falling edge of the reset signal (rst_n), the module calculates the final product.
If the reset signal (rst_n) is low, indicating a reset condition, the product output (mul_out) is set to 0.
If the reset signal (rst_n) is high, the product output (mul_out) is updated with the sum of registers.

samples.xml_description:
Implement a perpetual calendar with seconds, minutes, and hours in combinational logic. Clock input Active high reset signal 6-bit output representing the current hours (0-23). 6-bit output representing the current minutes (0-59). 6-bit output representing the current seconds (0-59). Seconds counter. Minutes counter. Hours counter.

Reset the seconds counter (Secs) whenever the reset signal (RST) is active.

Increment the seconds counter (Secs) at each clock cycle, wrapping around to 0 when it reaches 59.

Reset the minutes counter (Mins) whenever the reset signal (RST) is active.

Increment the minutes counter (Mins) when both the seconds counter (Secs) reaches 59 and the minutes counter (Mins) is less than 59, wrapping around to 0 when it reaches 59.

Reset the hours counter (Hours) whenever the reset signal (RST) is active.

Increment the hours counter (Hours) when both the seconds counter (Secs) and minutes counter (Mins) reach 59, wrapping around to 0 when it reaches 23.

samples.xml_description:
Implement an asynchronous FIFO, FIFO bit width and depth can be configured(parameter DEPTH = 16, parameter WIDTH = 8). The asynchronous FIFO structure is divided into several parts. The first part is dual-port RAM, which is used for data storage. Instantiate dual-port RAM as a submodule, The RAM ports are input wclk, input wenc, input [$clog2(DEPTH)-1:0] waddr, input [WIDTH-1:0] wdata, input rclk, input renc, input [$clog2(DEPTH)-1:0] raddr, output reg [WIDTH-1:0] rdata. The second part is the data write controller. The third part is the data read controller. The fourth part is the read pointer synchronizer. The read pointer is collected using the two-stage trigger of the write clock and output to the data write controller. The fifth part is the write pointer synchronizer, which uses the two-stage trigger of the read clock to collect the write pointer and output it to the data read controller. Write clock. Read clock. Reset. Write increment. Read increment. Write data. Write full. Read empty. Read data. Data width of FIFO. Depth of FIFO.

Asynchronous FIFO is composed of four parts:

  1. Dual-port RAM: Data storage module that supports simultaneous read and write operations. It has separate clock inputs for write (wclk) and read (rclk) operations, separate address inputs for write (waddr) and read (raddr) operations, a write enable input (wenc) and a write data input (wdata), a read enable input (renc) and a read data output (rdata).

  2. Data write controller: Module that manages the write operation. It determines when data can be written to the FIFO and controls the write operation based on the write increment signal (winc) and the current state of the FIFO.

  3. Data read controller: Module that manages the read operation. It determines when data can be read from the FIFO and controls the read operation based on the read increment signal (rinc) and the current state of the FIFO.

  4. Read pointer synchronizer: Module that synchronizes the read pointer with the write clock. It uses a two-stage trigger of the write clock to collect the read pointer and output it to the data write controller.

  5. Write pointer synchronizer: Module that synchronizes the write pointer with the read clock. It uses a two-stage trigger of the read clock to collect the write pointer and output it to the data read controller.

Asynchronous FIFO uses Gray code to compare the read and write pointers. When the read and write pointers are equal, the FIFO is empty. When the write pointer has one more cycle RAM than the read pointer, the highest and second-highest bits of the read and write pointer are opposite, the remaining bits are the same, and the FIFO is full.

module signal_generator
(
    input clk,
    input rst_n,
    output reg [4:0] wave
);
    reg state;
    always @(posedge clk or negedge rst_n) begin
        if (!rst_n) begin
            state <= 1'b0;
            wave <= 5'b0;
        end
        else begin
            case (state)
                1'b0: begin
                    if (wave == 5'd31) begin
                        state <= 1'b1;
                    end
                    else begin
                        wave <= wave + 1;
                    end
                end
                1'b1: begin
                    if (wave == 5'd0) begin
                        state <= 1'b0;
                    end
                    else begin
                        wave <= wave - 1;
                    end
                end
            endcase
        end
    end
endmodule

module pulse_detect
(
    input clk,
    input rst_n,
    input data_in,
    output data_out
);

    reg [1:0] state;

    always @(posedge clk or negedge rst_n) begin
        if (!rst_n) begin
            state <= 2'b00;
        end
        else begin
            case (state)
                2'b00: begin
                    if (data_in == 1'b1) begin
                        state <= 2'b01;
                    end
                    else begin
                        state <= 2'b00;
                    end
                end
                2'b01: begin
                    if (data_in == 1'b0) begin
                        state <= 2'b10;
                    end
                    else begin
                        state <= 2'b01;
                    end
                end
                2'b10: begin
                    state <= 2'b00;
                end
            endcase
        end
    end

    assign data_out = (state == 2'b01) && (state == 2'b10);

endmodule

<module id="pulse_detect">
    <description>Implement a 4-bit binary counter in combinational logic.</description>
    <ports>
        <input id="clk">Clock signal.</input>
        <input id="rst_n">Reset signal (active low).</input>
        <input id="data_in">Input signal.</input>
        <output id="data_out">Output signal.</output>        
    </ports>
    <parameter_description>
        <enum id="s0">Initial state.</enum>
        <enum id="s1">State 1.</enum>
        <enum id="s2">State 2.</enum>
        <enum id="s3">State 3.</enum>
        <enum id="s4">State 4.</enum>
        <enum id="s5">State 5.</enum>
    </parameter_description>
    <logic_description>
        <enum_value id="s0"/> 
        <enum_value id="s1"/> 
        <enum_value id="s2"/> 
        <enum_value id="s3"/> 
        <enum_value id="s4"/> 
        <enum_value id="s5"/> 
        <reg id="state" type="sequential_logic" width_description="[2:0]">
            <description>State of the pulse detection process.</description>
        </reg>
    </logic_description>
    <implementation>
        <enum_value id="s0">Initial state.</enum_value>
        <enum_value id="s1">Transition state.</enum_value>
        <enum_value id="s2">Pulse detected.</enum_value>
        <enum_value id="s3">Pulse ends.</enum_value>
        <enum_value id="s4">Pulse detected.</enum_value>
        <enum_value id="s5">Pulse ends.</enum_value>
        Always block for pulse detection and output generation.
        When the rst_n signal is low (negative edge), the state register is reset to the initial state, and data_out is set to 0.
        If the current state is the initial state (s0 or s3) and data_in is high (1), the state transitions to s1. If data_in is low (0), the state remains the same.
        If the current state is s1 and data_in is low (0), the state transitions to s2, indicating a pulse.
        If the current state is s2 and data_in is high (1), the state transitions to s4. If data_in is low (0), the state transitions back to s3.
        If the current state is s4 and data_in is high (1), the state transitions to s5. If data_in is low (0), the state transitions back to s3.
        If the current state is s5 and data_in is low (0), the state transitions back to s0. If data_in is high (1), the state transitions back to s0 and data_out is set to 1.
        For all other cases, the state remains the same and data_out is set to 0.
    </implementation>
</module>

module JC_counter
(
    input clk,
    input rst_n
);
always @(posedge clk, negedge rst_n) begin
    if (!rst_n) begin
        Q <= 64'b0;
    end else begin
        if (Q[0] == 1'b0) begin
            Q <= {Q[63:1], 1'b1};
        end else begin
            Q <= {Q[63:1], 1'b0};
        end
    end
end
endmodule

module RAM
#(
    parameter WIDTH = 6,
    parameter DEPTH = 8
)
(
    input clk,
    input rst_n,
    input write_en,
    input [($clog2(DEPTH))-1:0] write_addr,
    input [WIDTH-1:0] write_data,
    input read_en,
    input [($clog2(DEPTH))-1:0] read_addr,
    output reg [WIDTH-1:0] read_data
);
    reg [WIDTH-1:0] mem [DEPTH-1:0];
    integer i;
    initial begin
        for (i = 0; i < DEPTH; i = i + 1) begin
            mem[i] = {WIDTH{1'b0}};
        end
    end
    always @(posedge clk or negedge rst_n) begin
        if (!rst_n) begin
            read_data <= {WIDTH{1'b0}};
        end
        else begin
            if (write_en) begin
                mem[write_addr] <= write_data;
            end
            if (read_en) begin
                read_data <= mem[read_addr];
            end
        end
    end
endmodule
samples.xml_description:
Implement a dual-port RAM with a depth of 8 and a bit width of 6 bits, with all data initialized to 000000. It has two groups of ports, respectively for reading data and writing data, and read and write operations can be carried out at the same time. When the read_en signal is 1, the read_data of the corresponding position is read through the read_addr signal and output; When the write_en signal is 1, data is written to the corresponding position through the write_addr signal and write-data signal.. Clock signal used for synchronous operation. Active-low reset signal. Defined as 0 for reset and 1 for reset signal inactive. Write enable signal to initiate a write operation. Address for the write operation. Data to be written to the RAM. Read enable signal to initiate a read operation. Address for the read operation. Output signal representing the data read from the RAM. Width of the data. Depth of the RAM.

Define a 8*6 RAM array.

Trigger always block on the positive edge of the clock signal (posedge clk) or the negative edge of the reset signal (negedge rst_n).

On reset, indicated by !rst_n, all memory locations in the RAM array are cleared to 000000.

Check if the write_en signal is 1, indicating a write operation.

Use write_addr to access the corresponding memory location in the RAM array and assign the value of write_data to it.

Trigger always block on the positive edge of the clock signal (posedge clk) or the negative edge of the reset signal (negedge rst_n).

On reset, indicated by !rst_n, the read_data register is cleared to 0.

Check if the read_en signal is 1, indicating a read operation.

Use read_addr to access the corresponding memory location in the RAM array and assign the value to read_data.

If read_en is not 1, read_data register is cleared to 0.

samples.xml_description:
Implement a multi-bit MUX-based synchronizer. The data_en input is synchronous to clk_a and the data_in input is constant during the period when data_en=1. So, sample the data_in when data_en=1 and synchronize it to clk_b. The data change frequency of data_in is very low. The change interval between two adjacent data is at least 10 clk_b clock cycles. The clock clk_a is responsible for data_in and data_en. The clock clk_b is responsible for the 2 D flip-flops. Clock signal A. Clock signal B. Active-low reset signal A. Active-low reset signal B. Input data. Input enable. Output data. State one. State two. state_one state_two state_one state_one

Define a register data_reg to store the input data signal.

Define a register en_data_reg to store the input enable signal data_en.

Define two registers, en_clap_one and en_clap_two, to control the selection of the input data.

On the positive edge of clock signal A (clk_a) or the negative edge of reset signal A (arstn), update the data_reg and en_data_reg registers.

On the positive edge of clock signal B (clk_b) or the negative edge of reset signal B (brstn), update the en_clap_one and en_clap_two registers.

On the positive edge of clock signal B (clk_b) or the negative edge of reset signal B (brstn), assign the output data value.
